records.explanation: On a maxillary central incisor, the mesial contact is in the incisal third and the distal contact is slightly more cervical at the incisal-middle junction. The central-to-lateral contact is centered faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: Do not put both contacts at the same height just because the crown looks broad and nearly balanced.

records.explanation: The maxillary lateral incisor steps cervically from a mesial contact at the incisal-middle junction to a distal contact in the middle third. Both contacts are centered faciolingually in the course map.
records.commonTrap: The rounded distal corner can make the distal contact look closer to the incisal edge than it is.

records.explanation: The maxillary canine follows the same cervical step: mesial at the incisal-middle junction and distal in the middle third. The course faciolingual map keeps canine contacts in the middle third.
records.commonTrap: Do not use the cusp tip as the contact landmark; contacts sit on the proximal crown contours.

records.explanation: Both contacts of the maxillary first premolar are at the occlusal-middle junction in posterior terminology, and the course faciolingual map places premolar contacts in the facial third.
records.commonTrap: Posterior contacts move facially; do not center them buccolingually as you would an anterior contact.

records.explanation: The maxillary second premolar keeps both contacts at the occlusal-middle junction and in the facial third faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: Its rounder crown does not move the contact into the middle of the occlusal table.

records.explanation: The maxillary first molar has a mesial contact in the occlusal third and a distal contact in the middle third. Faciolingually, its broad mesial contact begins facially and extends toward the central groove, while the distal contact lies in the middle third.
records.commonTrap: Do not shrink the broad mesial contact to the facial third alone; the course map extends it toward the central groove.

records.explanation: The maxillary second molar repeats the course height map of mesial in the occlusal third and distal in the middle third. Its contact map is more central faciolingually than the premolars.
records.commonTrap: Do not carry the premolar facial-third rule unchanged onto every maxillary molar contact.

records.explanation: Tooth #1 is terminal in the maxillary right arch. It has a mesial neighbor (#2), but there is no tooth distal to it to form an interproximal contact in a complete permanent arch map.
records.commonTrap: Do not confuse a distal crown surface with a distal contact; a terminal tooth still has the surface but no distal neighbor.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.docx
sourceRefs.locator: Table 8, permanent arch sequence Study Rule

records.explanation: Both contacts of the mandibular central incisor are in the incisal third and centered faciolingually, matching its strongly symmetric crown form.
records.commonTrap: Do not import the maxillary central's incisal-to-junction step onto the mandibular central.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.docx

records.explanation: Both mandibular lateral incisor contacts remain in the incisal third, although the distal contact is described as more cervical within that third. The contacts remain centered faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: More cervical does not automatically mean the distal contact has crossed into the middle third.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.docx

records.explanation: The mandibular canine has a mesial contact in the incisal third and a distal contact at the incisal-middle junction. Both are centered faciolingually in the course map.
records.commonTrap: The distal crown bend does not move the contact all the way into the middle third.

records.explanation: Both mandibular first premolar contacts are at the occlusal-middle junction and in the facial third faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: The small lingual cusp does not pull the interproximal contact toward the lingual surface.

records.explanation: The mandibular second premolar keeps both contacts at the occlusal-middle junction, with both contacts placed in the facial third faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: A broad, molar-like occlusal table does not make its contact location molar-like in this course map.

sourceRefs.locator: slides 5-16, tooth identity and proximal views

records.explanation: The mandibular first molar places its mesial contact at the occlusal-middle junction and its distal contact in the middle third. Faciolingually, the distal contact approaches the facial aspect of the middle third.
records.commonTrap: Do not place the distal contact as high and facial as the mesial contact.

records.explanation: The mandibular second molar has its mesial contact at the occlusal-middle junction and distal contact in the middle third. Both contacts occupy the facial aspect of the middle third faciolingually.
records.commonTrap: Its symmetric occlusal outline does not make the mesial and distal contacts equal in height.

records.explanation: Tooth #32 is terminal in the mandibular right arch. It contacts #31 mesially, but has no distal neighbor to form an interproximal contact in a complete permanent arch map.
records.commonTrap: A terminal molar has a distal surface, but no distal interproximal contact without a distal neighbor.

sourceRefs.locator: Table 8, permanent arch sequence Study Rule
